entries.title: A Brief on To-Do Lists
entries.guidingSection: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed
entries.rawOfficialText:
Things to do today include exploring the history of the to-do list (alternate link). You will find that they were famously used by Benjamin Franklin and Leonardo da Vinci, among other high achievers of history. Below are some more modern approaches to to-do lists to explore - or even try. Which format seems best to you?
Eisenhower Method | 1-2-3 Method | Ivy Lee Method | 4-D Method | Eat that Frog | Must-Do | Bullet Journal

links.label: History of the to-do list - The Atlantic
History of the to-do list - archived alternate link
The Marginalian - A Brief History of the To-Do List and the Psychology of Its Success

entries.studentExplanation:
The main idea is not simply "make a list." A list can turn a big, confusing goal into smaller, clearer steps, but only if it helps you decide what matters most. The history link shows that people have long used lists to manage unfinished work, from famous historical figures to modern productivity systems. The Eisenhower Method sorts tasks by urgency and importance; the 1-2-3 Method limits the day to one big task, two medium tasks, and three smaller tasks; Ivy Lee reduces the next day to a short ranked list; the 4-D Method forces a choice between doing, delaying, delegating, or deleting; Eat That Frog says to start with the hardest important task; Must-Do / MoSCoW separates essential tasks from optional ones; and Bullet Journal turns lists into a flexible personal tracking system. Together, these systems show that planning is not about doing everything. It is about choosing a route through too much to do.

Add the practical methods as contrasting designs: Eisenhower sorts urgency and importance, Ivy Lee limits the day to six tasks, 1-2-3 balances big and small work, 4-D separates do/delay/delegate/delete, Eat That Frog attacks the hard task first, and Bullet Journal uses rapid logging to lower friction. Use this to distinguish planning from completion: what the list records, where attention gets stuck, how a method changes behavior, and why unfinished work keeps returning to the mind.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand that a to-do list is a way of transforming mental clutter into visible steps. At the section level, it opens More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed by showing that progress often begins with deciding what not to do. At the year-theme level, it connects to planning and progress because a list is a small map of a journey: it does not guarantee arrival, but it makes movement easier to see.
entries.takeaway: It makes progress visible while forcing choices
examples: Things to do today include exploring the history of the to-do list (alternate link)
Below are some more modern approaches to to-do lists to explore - or even try
Which format seems best to you
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether productivity systems genuinely help people move forward or simply create a feeling of control.
entries.counterargument: A to-do list can become another source of clutter if it records everything without forcing priorities.

items.title: History of the to-do list
items.note: List-making is old, cultural, and psychological - not just a modern app habit.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to look for a realistic image of a historical to-do list, notebook, Franklin virtue chart, or Leonardo-style notebook page. If /imagesearch cannot find a clean historical image, use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic WSC app-style desk scene with an old notebook, handwritten checklist, and modern checklist overlay. The image must be realistic, not anime, and inspired by the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, explaining the history or psychology of to-do lists. If no strong short video exists, use the article as a LINK button only.

entries.sectionTitle: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed

entries.title: The Zeigarnik Effect and the Done List
entries.guidingSection: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed
entries.rawOfficialText: It is easier to remember things we did not finish than those we did. That is why a song that got interrupted might nag at you until you hear the end of it, ooh na na - or why you might be unable to keep an overdue assignment out of your head even when you are taking a break. Psychologists term this the Zeigarnik Effect. Discuss with your team: how can we use the Zeigarnik Effect to our advantage in tasks like preparing for the World Scholar’s Cup? Would we be healthier if we spent more time remembering the things we got done - keeping a done list - than the things we did not?

links.label: Zeigarnik Effect - Psychology Today
Done list - Oliver Burkeman
Supplementary Zeigarnik explanation - Psych Central

entries.studentExplanation:
The Zeigarnik Effect explains why unfinished tasks can keep returning to your mind. An incomplete assignment, an interrupted song, or a half-finished WSC study plan can feel mentally open, so your brain keeps checking it. This can be useful because it pushes you back toward unfinished work, but it can also become stressful when too many open tasks compete for attention. The Psychology Today link adds a very practical idea: you do not always have to finish the task immediately to calm the mind; making a specific plan can help close the mental loop. The done-list link then flips the focus. Instead of only tracking what remains unfinished, a done list records what has already been completed, which can fight the feeling that you are always behind.

The source notes sharpen the mechanism: unfinished tasks stay mentally active because the mind keeps checking the open loop. A done list works differently from a to-do list because it records movement already made, which can reduce the feeling of productivity debt and help students see progress instead of only lack. Use this to distinguish planning from completion: what the list records, where attention gets stuck, how a method changes behavior, and why unfinished work keeps returning to the mind.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this explains why incompletion feels psychologically powerful. At the section level, it shows that planning is not only about external lists, but also about memory, anxiety, and attention. At the year-theme level, it connects to delay and progress because unfinished journeys can motivate us, exhaust us, or become easier to handle when we give them structure.
entries.takeaway: Unfinished work needs structure, not obsession
examples: It is easier to remember things we did not finish than those we did
Psychologists term this the Zeigarnik Effect
Closing the mental loop with a plan
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether unfinished work should be used as motivation or balanced with healthier ways to recognize completed progress.
entries.counterargument: The tension of unfinished work can motivate action, but it can also increase anxiety when too many loops stay open.

entries.sectionTitle: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed

entries.title: Umberto Eco, the To-Do List Specialist
entries.guidingSection: More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed
entries.rawOfficialText: “The list is the origin of culture,” the writer Umberto Eco once said. He held an exhibit of lists at the Louvre (which should have improved security on its own to-do list); he compiled a book on the topic, too. Learn about Eco’s distinction between practical and poetic lists and consider - what kind of list is a to-do list? Then discuss with your team: are we more drawn to lists than we should be?

links.label: Umberto Eco and lists - The Guardian

entries.studentExplanation:
Umberto Eco’s idea is that lists do more than organize chores. In The Infinity of Lists, he distinguishes between practical lists and poetic lists. Practical lists are useful and limited: shopping lists, schedules, inventories, guest lists, and to-do lists help people organize real life. Poetic lists are more imaginative: they can suggest abundance, wonder, excess, or something too large to fully contain. A to-do list is mostly practical, but it can also become strangely poetic when it grows endlessly and starts to represent a whole life of unfinished goals. Eco’s point helps students see that lists are not only productivity tools; they are one way humans try to make order inside infinity.

Eco’s point is bigger than errands. Lists can organize culture, imagination, collecting, knowledge, and infinity; they let people handle things too large to hold in memory. Students should compare practical lists, poetic lists, museum-like lists, and impossible lists that reveal desire more than completion. Use this to distinguish planning from completion: what the list records, where attention gets stuck, how a method changes behavior, and why unfinished work keeps returning to the mind.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand that list-making belongs to culture, literature, and philosophy as much as to productivity. At the section level, it completes More To Do Than Can Ever Be Listed by showing the double nature of lists: they can help us act, but they can also reveal that there is always more to do. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and planning because lists are small attempts to answer “Are we there yet?” in a world where the answer is often “not yet, and maybe never completely.”
entries.takeaway: Lists help humans order abundance and imagine infinity
examples: “The list is the origin of culture,” the writer Umberto Eco once said
he compiled a book on the topic, too
Then discuss with your team: are we more drawn to lists than we should be
What kind of list is a to-do list?
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether lists are practical tools, cultural obsessions, or ways humans try to order abundance and uncertainty.
entries.counterargument: Most everyday lists are still ordinary practical tools; reading too much philosophy into them can overstate their importance.

visualSections.title: Umberto Eco, the To-Do List Specialist

items.title: Practical lists
items.note: Practical lists are useful, finite, and connected to real-world tasks.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic WSC app-style image showing several practical lists on a desk: shopping list, schedule, class assignment list, and inventory. It must be realistic, not anime, and inspired by the WSC app UI; WSC app mandatory. Use /imagesearch only if a clean museum/archive image of historical lists is found.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes explaining practical lists, productivity, or list-making. If no strong video exists, no video is required.

links.label: Umberto Eco and lists - The Guardian

items.title: Poetic lists
items.note: Poetic lists suggest abundance, excess, imagination, or infinity rather than just a task to complete.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a visual related to Umberto Eco’s The Infinity of Lists, his Louvre exhibition, or a relevant book-cover/exhibition image. If using a known book cover or exhibition image, use the exact source visual. If no usable image is found, use /imagecreator or /imagegen to make a realistic WSC app-style image of an open book spilling into a long, beautiful list of words and objects. Must be realistic, not anime, WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes about Umberto Eco, The Infinity of Lists, or why humans like lists. If no appropriate short educational video exists, use the Guardian link as the main LINK button.

links.label: Umberto Eco and lists - The Guardian

items.title: Lists as order and control
items.note: Lists can organize the world but can also control, exclude, or overwhelm.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagecreator or /imagegen to create a realistic WSC app-style split image: one side shows a helpful organized checklist; the other shows an overwhelming endless list spilling off the page. Use /imagesearch only if a strong educational image about information overload or lists appears. The image must be realistic, not anime, and WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a clear educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes about information overload, choice overload, or why lists can both help and overwhelm people.

links.label: Umberto Eco and lists - The Guardian

items.title: What kind of list is a to-do list?
items.note: Eco's distinction is applied to the to-do list: practical at first, symbolic when it represents an endless future of unfinished life.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagecreator to make a realistic WSC app-style visual of a to-do list that begins as a neat practical checklist and gradually turns into a long winding path. This should connect list-making to the WSC journey theme. It must be realistic, not anime, and WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes on to-do lists, productivity, and why lists can become overwhelming. If no high-quality video is found, no video is required.
